_meta.notes: Concept-level seed graph. Epistemology track in detail with cross-domain service nodes and outbound connections to adjacent subdomains. Demonstrates the granularity principle, cross-domain porosity, and edge schema. Not comprehensive — pattern to replicate for other subdomains. All edges are pedagogical prerequisites unless noted. Replaces archived thinker-level v0.1.

nodes.label: Critical Thinking & Argument Analysis
nodes.description: Identifying premises and conclusions, evaluating validity and soundness, recognizing informal fallacies, distinguishing deductive from inductive reasoning. The entry point — everything else requires this.

nodes.label: The Socratic Method
nodes.description: Elenchus as systematic cross-examination. The practice of testing beliefs through questioning rather than assertion. Distinguishing what you think you know from what you can defend. The origin of philosophy as a method rather than a body of doctrine.

nodes.label: Platonic Forms
nodes.description: The theory that abstract, perfect, unchanging Forms (the Good, the Beautiful, Justice) are more real than their material instances. Participation, the Third Man regress, the divided line between knowledge and opinion. The first systematic claim that reality is not what it appears to be.

nodes.label: Platonic Epistemology
nodes.description: Knowledge as recollection (anamnesis). The distinction between knowledge (episteme) and mere opinion (doxa). The allegory of the cave as an epistemological metaphor. Why the senses deceive and only reason grasps truth. Sets the terms for all subsequent epistemology.

nodes.label: Syllogistic Logic
nodes.description: Aristotle's formal system of deductive inference. The syllogism as the basic unit of valid reasoning. Figures and moods. The first attempt to formalize the structure of argument independent of content. Dominant for two millennia until modern logic.

nodes.label: Aristotelian Categories
nodes.description: The ten categories (substance, quantity, quality, relation, etc.) as the fundamental ways being can be predicated. Substance as primary being — that which underlies all properties. The framework that organizes Western metaphysical vocabulary until Kant reinterprets it.

nodes.label: Aristotelian Teleology
nodes.description: The doctrine of four causes, especially the final cause — the 'for the sake of which.' Nature as purposive. Natural objects have inherent ends (telos). The acorn exists for the sake of the oak. This framework dominated natural philosophy until the Scientific Revolution dismantled it.

nodes.label: Euclidean Geometry as A Priori Knowledge
nodes.description: Geometry as the paradigm of certain, necessary knowledge about the physical world derived without experiment. From Plato through Kant, geometry was the strongest evidence that the mind can know truths about reality through reason alone. Kant's central question — how is synthetic a priori knowledge possible? — takes Euclidean geometry as its primary example.

nodes.label: The Scientific Revolution
nodes.description: The overthrow of Aristotelian natural philosophy by mechanistic explanation (Galileo, Newton). Nature as governed by mathematical laws, not purposes. The success of empirical method in generating reliable knowledge. Creates the epistemological crisis that motivates modern philosophy: if the senses can reveal natural law through experiment, what role remains for reason?

nodes.label: The Enlightenment as Intellectual Movement
nodes.description: The 18th century commitment to reason, individual autonomy, and progress. Sapere aude — dare to think for yourself. The conviction that human reason can ground morality, politics, and knowledge without appeal to tradition or revelation. The intellectual atmosphere in which Kant works and which his critical philosophy both expresses and transforms.

nodes.label: Knowledge, Belief, and Justification
nodes.description: The classical analysis of knowledge as justified true belief (JTB). What makes a belief count as knowledge rather than lucky guess? The structure of justification — foundationalism vs. coherentism. The regress problem: if every belief needs justification, what justifies the justifiers? Sets the conceptual vocabulary for all modern epistemology.

nodes.label: Ancient Skepticism
nodes.description: Pyrrhonian suspension of judgment (epochē) — for every argument there is an equal opposing argument. Academic skepticism — nothing can be known. The problem of the criterion: you need a criterion to evaluate knowledge claims, but the criterion itself needs evaluation. These arguments remain unrefuted; modern epistemology responds to them rather than solving them.

nodes.label: Cartesian Doubt & the Cogito
nodes.description: Descartes's method of doubt as a response to skepticism: doubt everything that can possibly be doubted. The dream argument, the evil demon. The cogito ergo sum as the one indubitable truth — the act of doubting proves the existence of a doubter. The founding move of modern philosophy: certainty must be rebuilt from a single secure foundation.

nodes.label: Cartesian Dualism
nodes.description: Mind (res cogitans) and body (res extensa) as two fundamentally different substances. The mind is non-spatial, indivisible, and known through introspection. The body is spatial, divisible, and known through the senses. The interaction problem: how do two utterly different substances causally affect each other? Generates the mind-body problem that philosophy of mind still addresses.

nodes.label: Innate Ideas & Rationalist Epistemology
nodes.description: The rationalist doctrine that certain ideas (God, infinity, mathematical truths, logical principles) are innate — present in the mind prior to any experience. Descartes, Spinoza, and Leibniz argue that reason, not sensation, is the primary source of knowledge. The senses are unreliable; the intellect grasps necessary truths that experience could never establish. The position Locke attacks.

nodes.label: Tabula Rasa & the Empiricist Turn
nodes.description: Locke's rejection of innate ideas: the mind begins as a blank slate. All ideas originate in experience — either sensation (from the external world) or reflection (the mind observing its own operations). There are no innate principles; what appears innate is merely learned so early it feels natural. The founding argument of British Empiricism.

nodes.label: Primary and Secondary Qualities
nodes.description: Locke's distinction: primary qualities (extension, shape, motion) belong to objects themselves and are measurable. Secondary qualities (color, taste, sound) are produced in the perceiver by the object's primary qualities but don't resemble anything in the object. Opens the door Berkeley walks through: if secondary qualities are mind-dependent, why not primary ones too?

nodes.label: Berkeleyan Idealism
nodes.description: Berkeley's radical conclusion: esse est percipi — to be is to be perceived. If we only ever experience ideas (not objects directly), there is no reason to posit material substance behind our perceptions. All that exists are minds and their ideas. God's continuous perception sustains the world. Pushes Locke's empiricism to its logical extreme and sets up Hume's further demolition.

nodes.label: Humean Skepticism
nodes.description: Hume's radical empiricism: all knowledge reduces to impressions (vivid sensory data) and ideas (faint copies of impressions). The problem of induction — we cannot rationally justify the inference from past to future. Causation is not a feature of the world but a habit of the mind (constant conjunction). Hume's fork: all propositions are either relations of ideas (certain but empty) or matters of fact (informative but uncertain). The skeptical crisis that awakens Kant from his 'dogmatic slumber.'

nodes.label: The Analytic-Synthetic Distinction
nodes.description: Kant's reframing of Hume's fork. Analytic judgments: the predicate is contained in the subject ('all bachelors are unmarried'). Synthetic judgments: the predicate adds something new ('the cat is on the mat'). Hume's fork assumed all a priori knowledge is analytic (empty tautology) and all synthetic knowledge is empirical (uncertain). Kant asks: what if some knowledge is both synthetic AND a priori — genuinely informative yet necessarily true?

nodes.label: Synthetic A Priori Knowledge
nodes.description: Kant's central claim: some knowledge is both informative about the world (synthetic) and knowable independent of experience (a priori). Examples: '7 + 5 = 12' (arithmetic), 'the shortest distance between two points is a straight line' (geometry), 'every event has a cause' (physics). Newtonian mechanics and Euclidean geometry demonstrate that such knowledge exists. The question 'how is it possible?' generates the entire Critique of Pure Reason.

nodes.label: Transcendental Idealism
nodes.description: Kant's answer to his own question: synthetic a priori knowledge is possible because the mind actively structures experience. Space and time are forms of intuition (how we receive data), and the categories are forms of understanding (how we organize it). We can know the world as it appears to us (phenomena) but never as it is in itself (noumena). The 'Copernican revolution' in philosophy: instead of the mind conforming to objects, objects conform to the mind's structure.

nodes.label: Kantian Categories of Understanding
nodes.description: The twelve pure concepts (unity, plurality, totality; reality, negation, limitation; substance, causality, reciprocity; possibility, existence, necessity) that the understanding necessarily applies to all experience. Kant derives them from the forms of logical judgment — transforming Aristotle's descriptive categories into conditions of possible experience. The transcendental deduction: proving that these categories must apply to any experience we could possibly have.

nodes.label: The Thing-in-Itself (Noumenon)
nodes.description: The object as it exists independently of our experience — unknowable by definition, since all knowledge is structured by our cognitive apparatus. The noumenal realm marks the limit of theoretical reason. Its existence must be thought (as the ground of appearances) but can never be known. Creates the central tension in Kant's system: if we cannot know things-in-themselves, what grounds morality, freedom, and God? The post-Kantian tradition begins by trying to resolve or dissolve this concept.

nodes.label: The Categorical Imperative
nodes.description: Kant's supreme moral principle: act only according to maxims you could will as universal laws. Morality grounded in reason alone, not consequences or sentiment. The formula of humanity: treat persons always as ends, never merely as means. Requires transcendental idealism because moral freedom is possible only in the noumenal realm — if we were entirely determined by natural causation (phenomena), moral obligation would be meaningless.

nodes.label: The Phenomenological Method
nodes.description: Husserl's program: return 'to the things themselves' by describing the structures of consciousness as it experiences objects. Intentionality — every consciousness is consciousness OF something. The phenomenological epochē (bracketing natural attitudes about the world's existence). Transforms Kant's transcendental project: instead of deducing conditions of possible experience, directly describe how experience presents itself.

nodes.label: Philosophical Foundations of Empirical Science
nodes.description: The philosophical questions that arise from the practice of empirical science. Can induction be justified? What distinguishes science from pseudoscience (demarcation)? How do scientific theories change (Popper's falsificationism, Kuhn's paradigms)? The problem of underdetermination. These questions are direct descendants of the rationalism-empiricism debate and Hume's problem of induction.

nodes.label: The German Idealist Response to Kant
nodes.description: Fichte, Schelling, and the early Hegel confront Kant's thing-in-itself as an intolerable remainder. If we cannot know it, we have no right to assert it exists. The Absolute Ego (Fichte), Nature-Spirit identity (Schelling), and the dialectical overcoming of subject-object dualism (Hegel) are all attempts to complete or surpass Kant's system by eliminating the noumenal limit. The launching point of continental philosophy.

nodes.label: The Problem of Introspection
nodes.description: Can the mind reliably observe its own operations? Descartes assumed yes — the cogito depends on transparent self-knowledge. Hume found no stable self to observe. Kant argued that we can know the forms of thought but not the thinking subject itself. When experimental psychology later tested introspection empirically, it fractured into competing schools (structuralism vs. functionalism vs. behaviorism). A service node for philosophy of mind.

edges.evidence: You need argument analysis to understand why Socratic questioning works as a method.
Formal logic extends informal argument analysis into a rigorous system.
The Forms emerge from Socratic questioning — the search for stable definitions leads to the hypothesis of eternal, perfect originals.
Platonic epistemology is the theory of how we access the Forms — recollection, the divided line, and the cave allegory are all epistemological consequences of the metaphysics of Forms.
Aristotle's categories are a direct response to Plato — replacing transcendent Forms with immanent structure (substance and its predicates).
The categories parallel the forms of predication. Understanding how predication works in the syllogism helps grasp why Aristotle identifies categories as modes of being.
Teleology depends on the category of substance — things have natural ends because they have essential natures (substantial forms).
The Scientific Revolution is fundamentally the rejection of teleological explanation in nature. You cannot understand what it overthrew without knowing Aristotelian teleology.
The Enlightenment's confidence in human reason is underwritten by the Scientific Revolution's success — Newton proved that reason and experiment can decode the universe.
Plato's epistemology establishes geometry as the paradigm of knowledge (the Meno, the Republic's divided line). Understanding why geometry was considered a priori knowledge requires this background.
The distinction between knowledge and opinion originates with Plato. The JTB analysis inherits and formalizes Plato's terms.
Skepticism attacks the concept of justified belief directly. You must understand what the skeptic is targeting.
Descartes's method of doubt is a deliberate intensification of ancient skeptical arguments. The evil demon is a more radical version of Academic skepticism.
Dualism follows directly from the cogito — the mind is known with certainty, the body is not, therefore they must be distinct substances.
If the senses are unreliable (as doubt establishes), knowledge must come from ideas the mind already possesses. Innate ideas are the rationalist escape from skepticism.
Locke's tabula rasa is a direct rejection of innate ideas. Book I of the Essay is entirely devoted to refuting them. You must understand the position being attacked.

Locke's empiricism is partly motivated by the Scientific Revolution's demonstration that observation and experiment produce reliable knowledge. Not strictly required to understand the argument, but a learner without this context will miss why empiricism felt compelling.
If all ideas come from experience, the next question is which ideas accurately represent external reality. The primary/secondary distinction is Locke's answer.

Berkeley's argument takes Locke's admission that secondary qualities are mind-dependent and extends it: primary qualities are also known only through ideas, so material substance is an unnecessary hypothesis.
Hume completes the empiricist demolition Berkeley began. If we have no access to substance (Berkeley) then we have no access to causation, self, or inductive reasoning either.

Hume's skepticism about causation extends the empiricist logic about qualities — if we only perceive impressions, not connections between them, causation is not observed but inferred (and the inference cannot be rationally grounded).

Kant's analytic/synthetic distinction is a direct reframing of Hume's fork. Without understanding what Hume divided (relations of ideas vs. matters of fact), Kant's recategorization is unintelligible.

The analytic/synthetic distinction turns on the logical relationship between subject and predicate. Familiarity with predicate logic helps, but a strong learner might grasp it without formal training.
Synthetic a priori is the third category that Hume's fork excluded and Kant claims exists. The distinction must be understood before the claim makes sense.
Newtonian mechanics is Kant's primary example of synthetic a priori knowledge in action. Without understanding what Newton achieved, the student won't grasp what Kant is trying to explain.
Euclidean geometry is Kant's other key example of synthetic a priori knowledge. Understanding why geometry seemed both necessarily true and informative about the world motivates Kant's question.
Transcendental idealism IS Kant's answer to 'how is synthetic a priori knowledge possible?' The answer is unintelligible without the question.
The categories are the specific content of transcendental idealism — the twelve concepts the understanding necessarily applies to structure experience.
Kant explicitly transforms Aristotle's categories from descriptions of being into conditions of experience. The echo is deliberate and understanding Aristotle's version illuminates Kant's revision.
The thing-in-itself is the necessary complement of transcendental idealism — if we only know phenomena as structured by our cognition, there must be a noumenal reality we cannot access.
The categorical imperative requires transcendental idealism because moral freedom is only possible in the noumenal realm. Without the phenomena/noumena distinction, Kant cannot ground moral autonomy.
Moral freedom depends on the noumenal self — the self as it is in itself, not as it appears in the phenomenal causal order.

Husserl's phenomenology transforms Kant's transcendental project. Instead of deducing conditions of possible experience, Husserl directly describes how experience presents itself. Unintelligible without Kant's framework.
Husserl's intentional structures parallel Kant's categories but are discovered through description rather than deduction. Understanding the categories helps but may not be strictly required.
German Idealism begins with the scandal of the thing-in-itself. Fichte, Schelling, and Hegel all define their projects by their response to this concept.
Hegel's dialectical categories are a transformation of Kant's table. Understanding what Kant was doing with the categories illuminates what Hegel is doing differently.
Hume's problem of induction is the founding problem of philosophy of science. Every major figure (Popper, Kuhn, Lakatos) is responding to it.
Philosophy of science examines the practice the Scientific Revolution inaugurated. You must understand what science does before you can ask philosophical questions about how it works.

The problem of introspection inherits Descartes's assumption that the mind is transparent to itself. Understanding why he assumed this — and why subsequent philosophy questioned it — requires dualism as background.

Kant works within and transforms Enlightenment assumptions about reason's authority. Not strictly required to follow the arguments of the Critique, but a learner without Enlightenment context will miss why the project mattered.
